title: Digestive biscuits
subtitle: Wholemeal and oat biscuits, docked and baked until sandy
excerpt: Twenty homemade digestive biscuits: wholemeal flour and fine oats rubbed with cold butter, brought together with a little milk, rolled thin, docked all over with a fork, and baked until sandy and golden. The flavour is distinctly wheaty and slightly sweet, the template for a chocolate digestive or any biscuit base.
sourceNotes: Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the oaten biscuit section documents a rolled biscuit made from oatmeal and flour, rubbed with butter and bound with milk, which predates the commercial digestive form. The British digestive biscuit as documented in late Victorian domestic cookery uses wholemeal flour, fine oatmeal or rolled oats, butter, and a small amount of sugar, baked until dry and slightly sandy. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the plain biscuit chapter documents docking raw biscuit dough with a fork to prevent puffing. The recipe here follows this public-domain domestic method.

content.text: A homemade digestive has a nuttier, more wheaty flavour than the commercial version, partly because the wholemeal flour is proportionally higher, and partly because the biscuit is thicker. The texture should be sandy and short, not crunchy. The method is rubbing-in: cold butter worked into flour and oats until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs, bound with a little milk.
the raw dough with a fork prevents the biscuits puffing during baking.

attrs.body: Melt 100 g of dark or milk chocolate and spread a thin layer over the flat side of each cooled biscuit. Leave chocolate-side up on baking paper to set. The chocolate digestive made this way is better than any commercial version.
attrs.title: Chocolate digestive

items.name: Rolled oats
items.prepNote: blitzed briefly in a food processor to a coarser-than-flour texture

content.text: Combine the {{wholemeal-flour}}, {{plain-flour}}, blitzed {{rolled-oats}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter and rub in with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with no visible chunks of butter. Add the {{whole-milk}} cold milk and press the dough together until it just holds. It should feel like firm play-dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan and line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the chilled dough on a lightly floured surface to 4 to 5 mm thick. Cut into rounds with a 7 cm cutter and transfer to the lined trays. Dock each biscuit all over with a fork in a regular pattern of holes spaced about 1 cm apart. Gather and re-roll the scraps.

Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until golden at the edges and dry to the touch. They will still feel slightly soft at the centre but will crisp as they cool. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack. Completely cooled biscuits should snap cleanly and have a sandy, short texture. Store in an airtight tin for up to 10 days.
